key_insight: The paper extends large-deformation contact FEM to a fully coupled chemo-mechano-thermodynamical setting, enabling simulation of bonding and debonding driven by mechanics, chemistry, and heat.
engineering_relevance: This provides a versatile monolithic framework for predicting adhesion, interfacial reactions, and thermal effects in bonded structures, with direct relevance to manufacturing, joints, and material interfaces.
method: Builds on a six-field contact theory with deformation and temperature in both bodies plus interfacial bonding and interfacial temperature.
Uses a quadratic contact potential to model bonding/debonding laws such as pressure- or gap-dependent adhesion, exothermic reactions, and thermal hardening/expansion.
Implements a fully linearized monolithic FEM with implicit time integration and classical/isogeometric shape functions; local condensation is possible for material-point bonding sites.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports several challenging illustrative examples demonstrating pressure- and gap-dependent bonding, exothermic reactions, thermal effects, and simultaneous bonding/debonding, but does not provide quantitative benchmark data in the summary.
watch_item: The formulation is mathematically and computationally complex, so robust Newton convergence, parameter calibration, and consistent linearization will be important in practice.
paper_title: A coupled finite element formulation for chemo-mechano-thermodynamical contact and its application to bonding and debonding
